coredata.dc:title: Construction of photocatalytic coating for alleviating the shriveling of postharvest fruit cucumber after simulated transportation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lihui
Zhang, Min
Mujumdar, Arun S.
Ma, Yamei
coredata.dc:description: The shriveling of fruit cucumber was commonly occurred during supply chain, photocatalyst exposed to UV light can endow the coatings with ethylene removal capacity to reduce the respiration of fruit and water loss. The study developed a novel photodynamic technology responsive photocatalytic coating with exceptional ultraviolet (UV) photocatalytic degradation of ethylene ability to decay the shriveling of postharvest fruit cucumber during supply chain. This coating involved the integration of Carbon dots (CDs)-loaded nano ZnO and the skillful selection of pullulan (Pul) and apple pectin (AP) matrix. The CDs/ZnO coatings boasted an impressive array of photocatalytic degradation of ethylene and adhesion properties, including high ethylene removal rates of 32.04 % in 60 min UV light stimulation. The decrease of cell-wall strength, degradation of the cell wall polysaccharides and water loss resulted in cucumber shriveling. Compared with CK sample, after UV-CDs/ZnO coating treatment, the higher firmness and cell wall polysaccharides were found in cucumbers with lower cell wall degrading enzymes activities, weight loss and water movement, which was associated with the decrease of respiration and ethylene accumulation. The UV-CDs/ZnO coatings possessed promising potential for alleviating the shriveling of postharvest fruit cucumber and applications in fruits preservation in the future.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The shriveling of fruit cucumber was commonly occurred during supply chain, photocatalyst exposed to UV light can endow the coatings with ethylene removal capacity to reduce the respiration of fruit and water loss. The study developed a novel photodynamic technology responsive photocatalytic coating with exceptional ultraviolet (UV) photocatalytic degradation of ethylene ability to decay the shriveling of postharvest fruit cucumber during supply chain. This coating involved the integration of Carbon dots (CDs)-loaded nano ZnO and the skillful selection of pullulan (Pul) and apple pectin (AP) matrix. The CDs/ZnO coatings boasted an impressive array of photocatalytic degradation of ethylene and adhesion properties, including high ethylene removal rates of 32.04 % in 60 min UV light stimulation. The decrease of cell-wall strength, degradation of the cell wall polysaccharides and water loss resulted in cucumber shriveling. Compared with CK sample, after UV-CDs/ZnO coating treatment, the higher firmness and cell wall polysaccharides were found in cucumbers with lower cell wall degrading enzymes activities, weight loss and water movement, which was associated with the decrease of respiration and ethylene accumulation. The UV-CDs/ZnO coatings possessed promising potential for alleviating the shriveling of postharvest fruit cucumber and applications in fruits preservation in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The non-climacteric fruit cucumber (Cucumis sativus L.) is cultivated as a highly prized fresh food in many parts of the world (Fan, Zhang, & Jiang, 2019). The cucumbers are often consumed in fresh state, the moisture content of cucumber reaches 96%, which are susceptible to tissue shriveling, softening and decay (Adeboyejo & Oyesanya, 2023). Cucumber fruit is sensitive to low temperature, which is allowed to store in 10 °C and relative humidity of 90–95% (Zhang, Tang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2006). Postharvest transportation is a necessary procedure for cucumber fruit distribution, which is one of the major factors of cucumbers quality deterioration due to the susceptibility to damage during transportation (Fang, Zhang, & Wang, 2007). As reported by previous research, there were approximately 10–30% of losses occurred in the supply chain (Jiang, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Lim, 2014). Ethylene is released from fruits and vegetables through respiration, which can accelerate fruit ripening, lead to overripening and even decay, resulting in the decrease of shorter storage life of fruit (Huang & Zhang, 2012). Besides the loss of glossiness and rotten, the weight loss to shriveling of cucumbers also decrease price when sold on a weight basis and fruit quality (Li et al., 2018). It is an urgent problem to alleviate shriveling of postharvest fruits and vegetables after transportation.

Nano ZnO photocatalytic technology has attracted much attention and research due to the safety and non-toxicity, economic and environmental protection, and stable chemical properties. Nano ZnO has been extensively used in a variety of subjects such as photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, photocatalytic degradation of wastewater and exhaust gases, antimicrobial and antivirus, and development of new energy materials (Bi, Qin, Cheng, & Liu, 2023; Firmansyah, Bakri, & Yulizar, 2023; Liang & Shi, 2023). This provides a certain reference basis for the application of nano ZnO in the field of fruit and vegetable storage and preservation. The band gap width of ZnO nanoparticles is 3.37 eV, which absorb UV light at wavelengths less than 387 nm (Bi et al., 2023; Han, Xiang, Meng, Dong, & Lv, 2023). The electrons in the valence band of ZnO will be driven to jump to the conduction band to form photogenerated electrons (e-), and at the same time generate holes (h+) in the valence band, in which ZnO is exposed to external light with energy equal to or greater than its forbidden band width. Photogenerated holes (h+) have strong oxidizing properties to oxidize and degrade most organic substances (Packialakshmi et al., 2023). The photogenerated electron and hole pairs generated by ZnO under UV excitation are easy to be compounded, and the average lifetime of the carriers is short, and these drawbacks limit its further development and application to a certain extent (Kalita, 2023). The photocatalytic activity of ZnO can be improved by modifying ZnO to broaden its photoresponse range and enhance its ability to inhibit electron and hole pair complexation (Mustafa, Srivastava, Kashif Aziz, Kanaoujiya, & Rajkumar, 2023). Loading other materials on the surface of ZnO, such as composite with carbon-based materials (e.g., activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene), is one of the more feasible methods to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of ZnO (Han et al., 2023). Carbon dots (CDs)/ZnO as a composite is conducive to separate photo-generated electron-hole (e--h+) pairs, while also enhancing visible-light response due to the complementary energy level structures and inherent qualities (Han et al., 2023). As a result, it is anticipated that the CDs/ZnO composite will perform remarkably well in photocatalysis.

Coatings are utilized as passive and inactive barriers to defend produce against the detrimental impacts of chemical and mechanical forces (Huang, Zhang, Yan, Mujumdar, & Sun, 2009). On the other hand, coatings can modify the moisture, O2, CO2 and ethylene transfers, and decrease mechanical damage to produce structural integrity of fresh produce (Huang, Hong, Wang, Meng, Ke, & Kou, 2023; Wu, Zhang, & Wang, 2012). Furthermore, coatings are considered as a barrier for gases and moisture transfers around the fresh produce to control shriveling of fruit and vegetables, extending the shelf life of products (Panahirad et al., 2021; Yuan, Wang, Gao, & Chen, 2023).

However, there are few reports about the influence of UV light responsive photocatalytic (CDs/ZnO) coating on the shriveling of postharvest fruit cucumber during transportation and storage. Therefore, the purpose of the current study was (1) to prepare CDs/ZnO composite and characterize the properties of CDs/ZnO composite, and (2) to explore the effect of UV light responsive photocatalytic (CDs/ZnO) coating on the storage shriveling properties of cucumbers after simulated transportation, and (3) to evaluate the ability of UV light responsive photocatalytic (CDs/ZnO) coating to alleviate the shriveling properties of cucumbers, and its underlying regulatory mechanism.

Cucumbers (Cucumis sativus L. cv. ‘Tony 102′) with uniform size and weight (97.32 ± 2.65 g) were purchased from local supermarket in Wuxi, China. Nano zinc oxide (ZnO) and ascorbic acid were purchased from Baima Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China) and Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively. Apple pectin (AP) and pullulan (Pul) were purchased from Shanghai Xintai Industrial Co., Ltd and Beijing Innochem Technology Co., Ltd., respectively.

The deionized water containing 10% (w/w) ascorbic acid were reacted using a hydrothermal reactor (KH-50ML-E317, Xi'an Yibeier Instrument Equipment Co., Ltd) at 200 °C for 3 h (Fig. S1(a)). Subsequently, the solution was cooled and centrifuged at 6797 × g for 20 min, the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter. The filtrate was separated further by a dialysis bag (500 Da), then freeze-dried to obtain CDs for further experiment.

The CDs/ZnO composite were synthesized according to the hydrothermal method. CDs (1%, 2%, 4%) was dissolved in 100 mL 4% ZnO solution, stirred at 1000 rpm for 30 min using a magnetic stirrer (KX-85-2AS, Jiangsu Kexi Instrument Co., Ltd). The mixture solution was reacted in a hydrothermal reactor at 200 °C for 2 h. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged at 6797 × g for 20 min, the sediment was washed by deionized water until the supernatant became transparent. The sediment was freeze dried to obtain CDs/ZnO-1, CDs/ ZnO-2 and CDs/ZnO-3 composite (Fig. S1(b)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, ZnO and CDs/ZnO composite
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size of CDs, ZnO and CDs/ZnO composite were performed by a Zetasizer Nano ZS-90 (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK). Morphology of CDs and CDs/ZnO composite was observed using a transmission electron microscope (HT7000 Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was determined after preparation of a tablet (sample: KBr, 1:100) using a FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Equ inox 55, Karlsruhe, Germany). The crystallization of CDs and CDs/ZnO composite was determined using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (D2 PHASER, BRUKER AXS GMBH, Germany). The UV–vis absorption measurement was performed after suitable dilution (0.1 mg/mL) using a UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1800, Kyoto, Japan).

1 g ZnO or CDs/ZnO was dispersed uniformly on a slide and then placed in a 50 L photocatalytic reactor at 5 ± 1 °C and 90 ± 5% RH for the photodegradation of ethylene. First, 10 mL of high purity ethylene was extracted from the photocatalytic reactor with a syringe, and the fan was turned on to equilibrate the adsorption in a closed environment for 4 h. Then, 10 mL of gas was extracted from the photocatalytic reactor with a syringe, and the concentration of ethylene was measured by gas chromatograph (Shimadzu, Japan) -flame-ionization detector (Varian 3300; Varian, Palo Alto, CA, USA) (GC-FID). The initial concentration of ethylene in this study was 0.25 × 10-3 mg/m3. The three UV lamps were turned on at the same time, and the test was performed every 20 min for 2 h.

The conditions for the detection of ethylene by gas chromatography with a 2 m HayeSep T100/120 column (Grace Davison Discovery Sciences, Deerfield, IL, USA) were 100 °C of column temperature, 120 °C of the inlet temperature, and 150 °C of the FID detector temperature. The gas flow rate was 150 mL/min for the combustion gas, 22 mL/min for the gas hydrogen, and 27 mL/min for the carrier gas nitrogen, and the quantification method was the area external (26.7 μg/mL) standard method with a quantification volume of 1 mL and an injection volume of 100 μL and a stopping time of about 14 min.

CDs/ZnO coatings were prepared using different variations of concentrations of Pul and AP. The concentration of Pul and AP was set as 3% (w/v), the ratios of Pul/AP (w/w) were 10:0, 8:2, 6:4, 4:6, 2:8, 0:10, and the corresponding coating were coded as Pul, Pul/AP-1, Pul/AP-2, Pul/AP-3, Pul/AP-4 and AP, respectively.

The 0.5% CDs, 0.5% ZnO and 0.5% CDs/ZnO was dissolved in optimized Pul/AP solution according to adhesion properties, and stirred at 1000 rpm for 30 min using a magnetic stirrer (KX-85-2AS, Jiangsu Kexi Instrument Co., Ltd), which was set as CDs coating, ZnO coating and CDs/ZnO coating, respectively.

The method of Huang et al. (2023) was employed to measure the adhesion properties of coatings. Pul, Pul/AP-1, Pul/AP-2, Pul/AP-3, Pul/AP-4 and AP coatings solutions were stained with 0.1% Allura Red to analyze the adhesion properties on various surfaces. 1 mL of stained coatings was dropped on a glass plate with spreading over the plate. After drying at room temperature for 12 h, the dried glass plate with coatings was placed into 150 mL of deionized water to obtain the dissolution of coatings on the glass plate, the absorbance of mixture was determined every 20 min at 501 nm to draw the adhesion ability curves of Pul/AP coating on the glass plate. Additionally, a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) plate was coated with 0.2 mL of Pul/AP coating solution stained with 0.1 % Allura Red, and the plate was then tilted 45° to cause the Pul/AP coating to slide along the plate to monitor the flow trajectory and the amount of remaining coating on the plate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Postharvest treatment, simulated transportation and storage
ce:section.ce:para: Two fresh cucumbers (each group contained 30 cucumbers) were immersed in 300 mL 0.5% CDs coating, 0.5% ZnO coating and 0.5% CDs/ZnO coating solution for 30 s, and left at room temperature under a running ceiling fan for drying, which were served as CDs coating, ZnO coating and CDs/ZnO coating, respectively. The coatings were formed over the whole cucumber surface. Uncoated group was immersed in 300 mL sterile distilled water for 30 s (Fig. S1(c)), which were served as CK-2. Afterwards, the cucumbers were packaged in polyethylene terephthalate (PET) film bag with modified atmospheric packaging (5% O2, 5% CO2 and 90% N2). The cucumbers after immersing in sterile distilled water were packaged in PET bag without modified atmospheric packaging, which were served as CK-1. Packaged cucumbers were placed in a plastic frame, which could hold about 3 kg of cucumber. According to previous study (Zhang, Zhang, Law, & Ma, 2023), cucumbers was simulated transportation under 1.5 Hz frequency of vibration at 9 ± 1 °C and 90 ± 5% of relative humidity (RH) for 2 h using a vibration test set (Fig. S2) (Qingdao Rongle Technology Information Service Co., Ltd, Qingdao, China).

UV light device (254 nm, 20 W, Dongguan Minghan Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd) was fixed on the upper panel of the refrigeration. The distance between UV light device and samples was set as 43 cm, a light intensity illuminated to cucumber was 75 µW/cm2. For UV light and UV-CDs/ZnO coating group, the cucumbers were performed under the same operation parameters mentioned above of CK-1 and CDs/ZnO coating group, respectively, and then treated with UV light for 1 h (according to the result of section 2.5) every day. All cucumbers were stored at 9 ± 1 °C and 90 ± 5% of RH for 25 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Respiration rate and ethylene production of cucumber
ce:section.ce:para: Respiration rate and ethylene concentration were determined by a headspace gas analyzer (DK-190, Jinan Saicheng Electronic Technology Co., Ltd) and portable ethylene detection alarm instrument (TD400-SH-C2H4, Beijing Tiandi Shouhe Tech Development CO., Ltd), respectively. For the measurement of respiration rate, one cucumber was placed in a 2.5 L container with properly sealed at 25 °C. After 1 h, the gas analyzer probe was inserted in the headspace of the container to obtain respiration rate (CO2 concentration). The separate blank container was served as blanks. For the measurement of ethylene concentration, the gas analyzer probe was inserted in the PET bag to obtain ethylene concentration. Respiration rate and ethylene concentration was derived as mg CO2 kg−1h−1 and mmol C2H4 kg−1h−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Firmness and weight loss of cucumber
ce:section.ce:para: Firmness of cucumber was carried out from fruit middle regions with a 2 mm diameter TA/2 cylindrical probe by a texture analyzer (TA. XTC-18, Shanghai bosin industrial development Co., ltd, Shanghai, China).

Weight loss of cucumber was determined fruit weight to calculate the alteration of weight at each storage time. The firmness (N) and weight loss (%) were performed from nine cucumbers in each replication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell wall polysaccharides components of cucumber
ce:section.ce:para: Protopectin (PTP) and water-soluble pectin (WSP) contents of cucumber were performed as described by carbazole colourimetry (Zhang et al., 2020), the galacturonic acid equivalents (g/100 g) were used to determine PTP and WSP.

According to the method of Zhang et al. (2023), 5 g cucumber was homogenized with 100 mL 80% ethanol, and boiled for 20 min. After cooling the mixture, the solution was centrifuged at 6797 × g for 20 min, and the sediment was mixed with 50 mL dimethysulphoxide at 4 °C for 12 h. Thereafter, the solution was blended with 200 mL mixture that contained chloroform and ethanol (2:1, v/v), then incubated for 10 min at room temperature. The residue was collected and washed using 200 mL acetone to obtain cell wall material.

Na2CO3 soluble pectin (SSP), hemicellulose and cellulose content were carried out as suggested by the approach of Xin, Chen, Lai, and Yang (2017) and Ali et al. (2022), and expressed as mg/kg. 300 mg cell wall material was dissolved in 10 mL distilled water and stirred continuously at 2000 rpm for 4 h. The above procedure was performed for twice, and the sediment was collected and re-suspended in 10 mL of 50 mmol/L cyclohexanetrans-1, 2-diamine tetra-acetate (CDTA) for stirring at 2000 rpm for 4 h. The residue was resuspended twice with CDTA as described above. Thereafter, the residual homogenate was dissolved in 10 mL of 50 mmol/L Na2CO3 solution (contained 2 mmol/L CDTA), the procedure was repeated three times to collect supernatant as SSP. The sediment was then suspended in 20 mL 100 mmol/L NaBH4 containing 4 mmol/L NaOH and centrifuged for 20 min at 6797 × g to collect supernatant, designated as hemicellulose. The sediment was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water and designated as cellulose. The hemicellulose and cellulose content were measured via anthrone method (Chen, Hung, Chen, & Lin, 2017).

2 g cucumber was homogenized with 8 mL acetate buffer (40 mM, pH 5.2), which contained 2% polyvinyl polypyrrolidone and 100 mM NaCl. After extraction of 1 h, the mixture was centrifuged at 6797 × g for 10 min to obtain the supernatant for determining enzymes activities. Polygalacturonase (PG), pectin methyl esterase (PME), cellulase, β-galactosidase (β-Gal) and β-glucosidase (β-Glu) were determined following the methods of Zhang et al. (2023), which were quantified as U/kg.

Low-field nuclear magnetic resonance analyzer (PQ001, Niumag, Shanghai, China) was employed to measure the water distribution of cucumber, the testing parameters of Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) were referred to Fan et al. (2019).

The cucumbers (1 mm × 1 mm) were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde solution for 48 h at 4 °C, then embedded in paraffin and stained with SafraninO-Fast Green after dehydration using a graded ethanol series. The micro-morphology was observed under a light microscope (E200, Nikon, Tokyo, Japan) at 40 × magnification (Zhang et al., 2020).

The results of experiment were performed at least in triplicates to express as means ± standard deviations. Significant analysis among samples at a significant level of 0.05 (p＜0.05) were performed by Tukey’s test using SPSS 20.0 software (Inc., IL, USA).

TEM image and particle size of CDs, ZnO and CDs/ZnO are illustrated in Fig. 1 . The CDs morphology displayed spherical forms with the with an average particle size of 6.42 nm at the range of 4–7 nm. ZnO presented cuboid crystalline with particle size of 34–44 nm. CDs/ZnO exhibited practically a core–shell nanostructure with a particle size of 21–28 nm. The presence of CDs nanoparticles on the ZnO surface was observed in CDs/ZnO composite. Each of the CDs/ZnO possessed an individually formed core–shell structure with CDs. The electrical interconnection of the CDs/ZnO particles with one another, which caused an increase in the transport of the photo-excited charge carriers to the photocatalyst surface, where oxidation occurred (Han et al., 2023).

Fig. 2 (a) exhibits FTIR of CDs, ZnO and CDs/ZnO. CDs sample exhibited the stretching vibration of OH and amino groups at 3430 cm−1, the stretching vibration of CH at 2927 cm−1. The other peaks at 1702, 1614, 1386 cm−1 were indexed to stretching vibration of CO and CC stretching vibrations, and COC bending vibrations, respectively (Wen, Li, Yan, & Su, 2023). The spectrum of ZnO exhibited a typical band at 3432 cm−1, corresponded to OH stretching (Cen et al., 2023). The absorption peak at 563 cm−1 represented assigned to Zn-O of ZnO (Xiao et al., 2022). Notably, the typical characteristic peaks of both CDs and ZnO were observed in all CDs/ZnO composite, indicating that the importation of CDs into ZnO had no effect on the structure of CDs/ZnO composite. However, the typical absorption peaks of CDs or ZnO in CDs/ZnO composite had shifted to lower or higher wavelengths than those of CDs or ZnO. This might be associated with the interaction between CDs and ZnO that produced a stable composite to decrease the overall energy (Rajender, Kumar, & Giri, 2018). The red shift of the OH peak might be caused by the strong hydrogen bonds between the substances (Zhang, Zhu, & Zhao, 2017).

Fig. 2(b) exhibits the XRD patterns of CDs, ZnO and CDs/ZnO with different concentration of CDs. The pattern of CDs has a strong peak centered at 18.22°, which was assigned to a lattice spacing of graphitic carbon, indicating that CDs exhibited a similar structure with graphite. For the XRD patterns of the CDs/ZnO composites, the structure of CDs/ZnO is nearly unchanged compared to ZnO. It was found that all the CDs/ZnO composites have the remarkable diffraction peaks at 2θ of 31.8°, 34.4°, 36.3°, 47.6°, 56.6°, 62.8° and 67.8°, which was attributed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) planes of the ZnO, which indicated the crystal structure of ZnO remained unchanged after the surface modification by CDs (Zhang, Zhang, Liu, & Fang, 2023). However, the regular stack of CDs was changed by the intercalation of ZnO (Chen et al., 2019).

In order to investigate the effect of CDs introduction on photocatalytic efficiency of ZnO, the optical absorption spectra of the CDs, ZnO and CDs/ZnO composite are exhibited in Fig. 2(c). ZnO surface with a large-band-gap showed a narrow light-response from 200 nm to 800 nm, while CDs/ZnO extended the optical absorption edge to longer wavelength region, which was caused by the formation of mid-gap states within the band gap of ZnO (Zhang et al., 2017). Compared to ZnO, the absorption intensity of CDs/ZnO was improved in the visible region, the absorption boundaries of the CDs/ZnO were obviously red-shifted, and the absorption intensity in the visible and near-infrared regions was obviously strengthened, which proved that loading the appropriate amount of CDs on ZnO could effectively broaden the spectral response range. This was attributed to the appropriate interface interaction between CDs and ZnO surface (Rajender et al., 2018). The light response of CDs/ZnO was might related to the electron transfer between ZnO surface and CDs sheet (Kim et al., 2015).

As revealed in Fig. 2(d), the degradation effect of CDs/ZnO on ethylene showed an increasing trend with increasing CDs addition, and the best degradation effect was exhibited when the CDs addition was 2% (CDs/ZnO-2). With the increase of CDs content to 4%, the degradation effect of ethylene showed a decreasing trend. This was due to the fact that CDs possessed a high conductivity, and the appropriate addition of CDs was able to separate photogenerated electron-hole and extend the lifespan of photogenerated carriers. If the content of CDs was too much, CDs instead became the complex center of charge carriers, promoted the complexation of photogenerated electron-hole, and shortened the lifetime of photogenerated carriers, thus resulting in the decrease of the photocatalytic degradation capacity of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 2(e), the degradation efficiency of ethylene of ZnO or CDs/ZnO greatly increased, which was attributed to the production of O2– and –OH under UV irradiation (He, Dong, Zheng, Jia, Shan, & Zhang, 2019). Photodegradation mechanism of CDs/ZnO is exhibited in Fig. 2(f), the electrons (e-) on the surface of ZnO moved from the valence band to the conduction band upon stimulation by UV radiation. Meanwhile, photogenerated holes (h) continued to produce electron hole pairs in the valence band. Superoxide radicals (O2 –) were created by the electron acceptor (O+ 2) trapping the electrons on the ZnO conduction band, while hydroxyl radicals (·OH) were produced by the photogenerated holes in the valence band of the ZnO. Ethylene was further oxidized by these potent oxidizing agents, resulting in the end products CO2 and H2O (Xu et al., 2023). The modification of ZnO by CDs improved the photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency and extended the photogenerated carrier lifetime, therefore enhancing the photocatalytic performance (He et al., 2019). According to the results of degradation efficiency of ethylene, CDs/ZnO-2 prepared by 2% CDs and 4% ZnO was used for further experiment.

In order to explore the adhesion properties of coating solution on cucumber skin, Pul/AP solutions (3%) with different ratio were stained red with Allura Red to observe the adhesion ability of the coatings on different surfaces. Fig. 3 shows the adhesion outcomes of dyed Pul/AP solutions on the surfaces of glass plates and PTFE plates. The residual amounts of Pul, Pul/AP-1, and Pul/AP-2 on the flow trace were higher than those of Pul/AP-3, Pul/AP-4, and AP after the equal volume coating solutions slipped on the PTFE plate (Fig. 3a), which indicated that the enhancement of AP amount increased adhesion. The phenomenon might be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bond between Pul and AP (Panahirad et al., 2021). The Pul/AP coatings on the naturally dried hydrophobic glass plates showed various levels of dissolution in water. The residual area of Pul/AP-3, Pul/AP-4 and AP on the glass plate were lower than other coatings after 30 min (Fig. 3b), which indicated the adhesion properties of Pul/AP-3, Pul/AP-4 and AP were better than others. Meanwhile, the dissolution rate of Pul/AP-3, Pul/AP-4 and AP in water were slower than those (Fig. 3c). Therefore, Pul/AP-3 coating was chosen for further experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of fruit cucumber shriveling characteristics
Respiration rate and ethylene production of cucumber
ce:section.ce:para: Respiration rate is one of the most important indicators to reflect metabolic activity of fruits and vegetables. As shown in Table 1 , compared to fresh cucumbers (0 d), respiration rate of all cucumbers on the 5th day decreased, whereas respiration rate of cucumbers increased gradually from 5th day to 20th day, then decreased again until the last storage period. In contrast to the CK-1 sample, the respiration rate of other samples exhibited significant decreasing trends after 5–25 days of storage. However, respiration rate of cucumbers with UV treatment are similar to those with CDs coating and ZnO coating treatment (p > 0.05). Overall, UV, coatings and UV-CDs/ZnO coating treatments could inhibit the respiration rate of cucumbers. Remarkably, UV-CDs/ZnO coating exhibited the best effect to retard the respiration rate of cucumbers during storage. Ethylene production of cucumbers obviously increased as storage period advanced (Table 1). However, ethylene production of CK-1 sample was highest with a clear peak on 15th day as compared with other cucumbers. Overall, cucumbers with UV-CDs/ZnO coating treatment showed lower ethylene production among other treatments. After 15 days, ethylene production of all cucumbers presented considerably decrease, especially for CDs/ZnO coating and UV-CDs/ZnO coating treatments. Coatings could regulate the exchange of gases without hindering the respiratory process of fruit and vegetables, which offered crucial barrier properties, including the preservation of texture through moisture resistance, reduction of oxidation and extension of shelf life via a gas barrier. On the another hand, the reactive hydroxyl groups produced by the cavities of CDs/ZnO in coatings under UV excitation have a reaction energy of 1686.1 kJ/mol, which is higher than the chemical bonding energies in various types of organic substances, such as CC (347.4 kJ/mol), CH (414.4 kJ/mol), CN (305.6 kJ/mol), CO (351.6 kJ/mol), HO (464.6 kJ/mol), NH (389.3 kJ/mol), CC (611.1 kJ/mol), CO (736.70 kJ/mol) (Packialakshmi et al., 2023). The photocatalytic degradation of ethylene was related to the functional groups or chemical bonds. The light waves required to break CC and CH in C2H4 were 196.1 nm and 289.7 nm, respectively (Kim, Hwang, & Hong, 2002). Therefore, CC was broken and ethylene undergoes direct photodegradation under the action of a UV light at a wavelength of 254 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Firmness and weight loss of cucumber
ce:section.ce:para: The firmness was considered to judge the shriveling and freshness of cucumbers (Zhang et al., 2023). The firmness of cucumber was decreased due to the degradation of cell structure caused by respiration (An, Zhang, Lu, & Zhang, 2006). Firmness of cucumbers during storage is depicted in Table 1, the firmness of all cucumbers decreased, which was consistent with the previous reports (Zhang et al., 2023). The firmness of CK-1 sample decreased most significantly during storage. Additionally, the firmness of CK-1 and CK-2 samples was smaller than those of samples with UV and coatings treatments. The phenomenon was attributed to coatings weakened the gas exchange (O2 and CO2), as well as the ethylene production of cucumber during storage. The respiration rate of coating cucumber was reduced, which delayed the ripen of cucumber, preventing the decrease of cucumber firmness during storage (Chen, Tian, Xu, Wu, Liu, & Jiang, 2023). In addition, coatings preserved the physiological injury of fruits and microbial invasion via forming protective skin. Similar trends were found in litchi fruit and fresh pistachios treated with CDs and ZnO nanocoating, respectively (Su et al., 2023; Taghipour, Ehtesham Nia, Hokmabadi, & Martínez-Gómez, 2023).

The weight loss is an essential indicator to characterize product quality, which was mainly caused by the respiration and transpiration of fruit and vegetables during storage (Chen, Zhang, Bhandari, & Guo, 2018). As exhibited in Table 1, the weight loss of cucumbers significantly increased during storage, although UV and coating treatments was significantly higher than that of CK-1 and CK-2 sample. In CK-1 sample showed severe shriveling with 12.26% of weight loss on 15th day. After 25 days of storage duration, UV-CDs/ZnO coating sample presented maximum weight loss (9.87%). The weight loss of cucumber occurred due to the damage of the cytoplasmic membrane, leading to the decrease of tissue firmness. Coatings formed a layer to inhibit the evaporation and diffusion of water, which declined gas exchange in cucumber skin stomata to prevent water evaporation and weight loss of cucumber (Taghipour et al., 2023). The addition of CDs, ZnO or CDs/ZnO in coating, weight loss of cucumber significantly reduced due to the antimicrobial properties of CDs, ZnO or CDs/ZnO. As exhibited in Fig. 4 , CK-1 sample presented serious shriveling and soften phenomenon at 20th day. Compared with CK-1 sample, CK-2 sample occurred slight shriveling and soften phenomenon. However, it has been not observed slight shriveling and soften phenomenon in treatment sample at 20th day. After 25 days, cucumbers treated with UV, CDs coating and ZnO coating exhibited slight shriveling with uneven surface, while fruit skin of UV-CDs/ZnO coating sample revealed a smooth and intact surface. The phenomenon indicated UV-CDs/ZnO coating was most effective treatment in suppressing the shriveling of cucumber in this study. The high-energy ultraviolet rays (UV) can effectively stimulate CDs/ZnO to make it photocatalytically active, exhibiting ethylene scavenging activities, and creating reactive oxygen species (ROS) to prolong the shelf life of cucumber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell wall polysaccharides components of cucumber
ce:section.ce:para: Shriveling of cucumber is directly associated with alteration in the composition of cell wall polysaccharides (Zhang et al., 2023). Although SSP and PTP content decreased gradually in all cucumber, UV and coating treatments maintained significantly higher SSP and PTP content during the most of sampled timepoints (Table 2 ). SSP and PTP content in CK-1 sample was 84.63 mg/kg and 210.74 mg/kg, respectively after storage for 25 days, while SSP and PTP content were as 199.47 mg/kg and 314.79 mg/kg, respectively in UV-CDs/ZnO treated cucumber. Compared to SSP and PTP, WSP content of cucumber increased with storage time (Table 2). The increase of WSP content were the largest during 10–20 d of storage. On 25th day, WSP content of CK-1 sample was 8.61%, 14.77%, 18.90%, 10.93%, 22.69% and 24.23% higher than it was in CK-2, UV, CDs coating, ZnO coating, CDs/ZnO coating or UV-CDs/ZnO coating samples, respectively. UV and coating treatments delayed the transformation of SSP and PTP to WSP. Hemicellulose and cellulose content in CK-1 sample was lower than these were in treatment samples throughout the entire period of storage. Cellulose content in UV-CDs/ZnO coating sample was 24.42 % higher than it was in CK-1 sample after 25 days. After storage for 25 days, cellulose content was declined by 71.93%, 62.27%, 47.33%, 39.65%, 43.70%, 37.41% and 29.34% in CK-1, CK-2, UV, CDs coating, ZnO coating, CDs/ZnO coating or UV-CDs/ZnO coating samples, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4, the obvious decayed cucumber (CK-1 sample) was observed at 25th day, which might be contributed to such reduction of cellulose content (71%). Some studies have reported that the level of WSP generally increased during cell wall degradation, while insoluble pectin, cellulose and hemicellulose content decreased (Xiong, Sun, Tian, Xu, & Jiang, 2023). Coatings could maintain the stability and integrity of the cell wall, which prevented the fruit from shriveling and softening by lowering respiration and gas exchange (O2 and CO2) (Panahirad et al., 2021). The photodegradation of ethylene by the CDs/ZnO added coatings required activation by UV rays. CDs/ZnO coatings under UV irradiation could not only improve the quality of postharvest fruits by scavenging ethylene but also could be delay the aging (Zhang & Rhim, 2022). UV and coatings declined the cell wall-degrading enzymes activity, which could protect the tissue firmness of cucumbers. Moreover, the alteration of pectin and cellulose content mainly contributed to the variation and rearrangement of the cell wall structure of cucumber during shriveling.

PME and PG are the primary hydrolytic enzymes involved in fruit shriveling. CX can promote the decomposition of cellulose and xyloglucan in fruit cell walls (Ali et al., 2022). β-Glu and β-Gal belong to glycosidases to cause pectin degradation, expediting the fruit shriveling (Xiong et al., 2023). As shown in Table 2, β-Gal, β-Glu, PME and PG showed lower activity before 5th day, while presented a gradual increase from 5th day to 25th day. The increment of β-Gal, β-Glu, PME and PG activities was significantly higher in CK-1 and CK-2 samples as compared with treatment samples. Furthermore, UV-CDs/ZnO coating sample had significantly lower CX activity in comparison with other samples. On 25th day, β-Gal, β-Glu, PME, PG and CX activity exhibited a significantly less increase in CDs/ZnO coating and UV-CDs/ZnO coating samples as compared with CK-1 sample. UV-CDs/ZnO coating treatment declined the various cell wall degrading enzymes activities to prevent the degradation of cell wall polysaccharides and dissolution of cell wall, delaying in cucumber shriveling and senescence.

The changes in water distribution of cucumber during storage is shown in Fig. 5 (a). The water distribution of cucumber exhibited a high signal intensity (red) in core area and the medium signal intensity (green) in inner pericarp and outer pericarp, which corresponded to the high water content and medium water content, respectively (Hua et al., 2022). In all cucumbers, water loss initially occurred in core area followed by inner pericarp and outer pericarp, and treatments retarded the water loss from the tissues. During late storage period, the signal of inner pericarp and outer pericarp in CK-1 sample changed to blue color, whereas CDs coating, CDs/ZnO coating and UV-CDs/ZnO coating sample occurred red and green proton density without blue proton density, in especial for UV-CDs/ZnO coating sample. The results indicated these treatments could delay the water loss of cucumber to varying degrees. UV-CDs/ZnO coating presented the best effect in delaying the water loss of cucumber. The enhancement in cell wall degrading enzyme activity could accelerate water migration and movement in the fruit, further promoting shriveling (An, Zhang, & Lu, 2007). The coatings formed a barrier on the surface of cucumbers, preventing the respiration of cucumber and diffusion of water in samples (Panahirad et al., 2021). On the other hand, UV-CDs/ZnO coating inhibited the ethylene accumulation of cucumber, which might decrease the water movement.

The microscopic cell structure of cucumbers on 1st and 25th day are revealed in Fig. 5(b) and Table S1. Fresh cucumber tissues (1 d) showed intact cell walls with tight intercellular junctions. After storage for 25 days, the cell structure of all cucumbers changed. The dissolved cell wall and large intercellular spaces were observed in CK-1 sample on 25th day, and individual cells were indistinguishable. The same phenomenon was observed in CK-2 sample. In contrast to the CK-1 sample, UV and coatings treatments decreased the enlargement of intercellular spaces and rupture of cell walls on 25th day. In cell tissue of UV-CDs/ZnO sample, little intact cells wall could be observed. UV and coatings could maintain the cell structure of cucumber, which may be due to the fact that the UV and coating treatments could retard the cell wall degrading enzymes activities to inhibit a loss of cohesion and enlargement of intercellular spaces. The denser cell walls with blue indicated the higher levels of cellulose and pectin in the cell-wall extracts (Jiang, Yin, Wang, Zhong, & Deng, 2022). However, the loosely arranged cells with poor integrity were reflected the degradation of cell wall. These results suggested that structural changes of cell wall played a critical role in cucumber shriveling. Previous report also proved that cucumber shriveling might be associated with the structural alteration and rearrangement of the cell wall during storage (Zhang et al., 2023). CDs/ZnO coatings under UV-A irradiation can not only improve the quality of postharvest fruits by scavenging ethylene but also can be delay the tissue degradation (Zhang et al., 2022).

Photocatalytic coating was prepared by adding CDs/ZnO into Pul/AP solution-forming coatings to improve the photocatalytic degradation of ethylene properties. Among the CDs, ZnO and CDs/ZnO with different mass ratios prepared in this experiment, the CDs/ZnO (1:2) coatings exhibited the most excellent light absorption capacity and photocatalytic degradation of ethylene properties. Moreover, the UV-CDs/ZnO coating significantly decayed the shriveling of postharvest fruit cucumber during supply chain. The respiration rate, ethylene production, weight loss and cell wall degrading enzymes activities were decreased after UV-CDs/ZnO coating treatment, and the decrease of firmness and cell wall polysaccharides content of cucumber were inhibited, as well as water movement was retard. Therefore, UV-CDs/ZnO coating can be effectively applied to alleviate shriveling of postharvest fruit cucumber with a potential for fruits preservation in the future.

Lihui Zhang analyzed the data and wrote the manuscript. Min Zhang provided the presented idea and verified the analytical methods. Arun S. Mujumdar conceived and designed the experiments. Yamei Ma reviewed and edit the manuscript. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.
